svg.text: The selection gap factorizes into taste alignment × candidate supply —
and the alignment factor is the earlier runaway warning
Each round the model writes six answers per item and a judge keeps the top two, which become training data. Three per-round readouts:
taste alignment = correlation, within one item, between the judge's scores for the six candidates and those candidates' risk;
candidate supply = standard deviation of candidate risk within the item; selection gap = mean risk of the kept two minus the mean of all six.
A. The gap factors: gap ≈ alignment × supply
OLMo risk model, K2 grid — 13 runs
Qwen risk model, K1 grid — 12 runs
B. The alignment factor warns earliest
correlation of each round-1 readout with the run's
remaining drift (final minus round-1 pool risk)
Supply alone points the other way: high-variance
two OLMo risk-model runs, K2 grid,
both under a frozen neutral judge
Seed 5's alignment bloomed while supply was
remained — the product alignment × supply is
the force. Settled seed 0's alignment also
Data: experiments/taste_alignment_predictor.json — 25 four-round keep-top-2-of-6 selection rollouts (13 with the OLMo risk model on the K2 grid, 12 with the
Qwen risk model on the K1 grid). Every number is recomputed from the per-round records in that file; risk is the pool's mean judged risk score in [0, 1].
